title: On Writing Well
excerpt: The definitive guide to nonfiction writing, teaching clarity, simplicity, brevity, and humanity through principles that have shaped generations of writers.
keyIdeas: Clutter is the disease of American prose — every unnecessary word weakens your message
Writing is a craft, not an art, learned through rewriting and ruthless editing
Style emerges naturally from confidence and authenticity, not from imitation
The lead is the most important sentence; the ending must resonate
Each nonfiction form (travel, memoir, science, business, sports) has its own conventions
The reader's time and attention are sacred — the writer's job is to make the transaction effortless
keyTakeaways: Strip every sentence to its cleanest components — cut every word that does no work
Write for yourself and one ideal reader, not a faceless mass audience
Read your work aloud to catch rhythm problems and hidden clutter
Unity of tone and purpose is essential — every element must serve the whole
Nonfiction can be literature — use narrative, scene, dialogue, and character
whoShouldRead: Aspiring nonfiction writers seeking a comprehensive craft manual
Journalists wanting to improve clarity and narrative power
Students who never learned the fundamentals of clear prose
Business professionals who write reports, emails, and proposals
Bloggers and content creators looking for timeless principles
whoShouldSkip: Fiction writers (the book explicitly excludes fiction craft)
Experienced professional writers seeking advanced techniques
Those looking for a grammar handbook rather than a style guide
People who want quick checklists over writing philosophy
